subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: 1 checkerboard (8x8 grid of alternating light and dark squares — the same board used for Chess). 12 dark checkers (one player's pieces). 12 light checkers (the other player's pieces). Total: 24 pieces and 1 board.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content: Orient the board so that each player has a dark square in their lower-left corner. All play takes place on the dark squares only — the light squares are never used. The board has 32 dark squares arranged in a diagonal pattern.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Each player places their 12 checkers on the dark squares of the three rows closest to them. This fills 12 of the 32 dark squares per player, leaving the two center rows (rows 4 and 5) completely empty. All pieces start as regular men (flat). Kings are created during play.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: The player with the darker-colored pieces moves first. Players then alternate turns. In tournament play, colors alternate between games.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content: On your turn you move one piece. Regular men move diagonally forward one square to an empty dark square. If an opponent's piece is diagonally adjacent to your piece and the square beyond it is empty, you must jump over it — this captures the opponent's piece and removes it from the board. If after landing from a jump you can immediately jump again with the same piece, you must continue jumping. A single turn can chain multiple jumps. The critical rule: if any jump is available to you, you must take a jump — you cannot make a regular non-jumping move instead.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
REGULAR MEN: Move diagonally forward only — one square at a time to an adjacent empty dark square. Capture by jumping diagonally forward over an adjacent opponent piece to the empty square beyond. Can chain multiple forward jumps in a single turn.

KINGS: When a regular man reaches the far end of the board (the opponent's back row), it is crowned a King. Mark it by stacking a second checker on top or flipping it. Kings move and capture diagonally in any direction — forward or backward — giving them far greater range and flexibility. A piece that is crowned during a multi-jump sequence stops immediately on the king row — its turn ends and the remaining jumps, if any, are forfeited.

FORCED CAPTURES: Jumping is mandatory. If you can jump, you must jump. If multiple jump options are available on your turn, you may choose which one to take, but you must take one of them. During a multi-jump sequence, you must continue jumping until no more jumps are possible with that piece.

REMOVAL: Captured pieces are removed from the board immediately after each jump in a sequence (not held until the full sequence ends). This matters in multi-jump turns because removing a jumped piece can open or close subsequent jump paths.

subtopics.title: Special Rules
subtopics.content:
FORCED CAPTURES: Jumping is mandatory. If any of your pieces can jump, you must jump — you cannot make a non-jumping move instead. If multiple jump options are available, you may choose which one to take, but you must take one. During a multi-jump sequence, you must continue jumping until no more jumps are possible with that piece.

KINGING: When a regular man reaches the opponent's back row (the farthest row from the player), it is crowned a King by stacking a captured piece or spare checker on top. Kings can move and capture diagonally in any direction — both forward and backward. A piece that is crowned during a multi-jump sequence stops immediately — its turn ends even if additional jumps would be available.

CAPTURE REMOVAL: Captured pieces are removed from the board immediately after each individual jump within a multi-jump sequence — not held until the sequence ends. This matters because removing a jumped piece can open or close subsequent jump paths in the same turn.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
WIN CONDITIONS: You win by either capturing all 12 of your opponent's pieces, or leaving your opponent with no legal moves on their turn (all remaining pieces are blocked). Both outcomes end the game immediately.

DRAWS: The game is drawn by mutual agreement, or under tournament rules if 40 consecutive moves pass without a capture or a man being crowned King. A draw can also occur when neither player has enough material or positional advantage to force a win — for example, when each player has a single King and neither can corner the other.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content: Move pieces toward the center of the board in the opening. Center pieces have more movement options and more jump opportunities than pieces on the edges. Advance a few pieces forward while keeping your back row intact for as long as possible — your back row pieces prevent your opponent from getting easy Kings. A strong opening advances 2-3 center pieces to rows 4 and 5 while maintaining a solid formation behind them.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
EMPTYING YOUR BACK ROW TOO EARLY: Your back row is your last line of defense. If you move all your back-row pieces forward, your opponent can slip a piece through to the king row for a free King. Keep at least one or two back-row pieces in place until you are ready to trade them strategically.

IGNORING FORCED CAPTURES: Because jumping is mandatory, your opponent can sometimes force you into bad captures by positioning a sacrifice piece. Before every move, look at what jumps your opponent will be forced to make — and set up positions where their mandatory jump leaves them exposed.

CHASING PIECES AROUND THE BOARD: Do not send a single piece on a solo mission deep into enemy territory. An isolated piece is easy to trap or force into an unfavorable jump. Advance pieces in coordinated groups where they support each other.

NOT THINKING ABOUT KING TRADES: When you have more Kings than your opponent, trade Kings one-for-one whenever you can. Each even trade brings you closer to a winning material advantage. When behind in Kings, avoid trades and look for ways to crown another piece.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
KING STRATEGY: Kings are dramatically more powerful than regular men because they move in all four diagonal directions. Getting a King while your opponent has none is often a decisive advantage. Sacrifice a regular man to get a King if the trade is worth it — one King is typically more valuable than two regular men in the endgame.

FORCED CAPTURE TACTICS: Since jumping is mandatory, you can exploit this rule by offering a sacrifice. Place a piece where your opponent must capture it, and the resulting jump lands them on a square where you can capture two or three of their pieces on your next turn. This two-for-one or three-for-one exchange is the most common tactical pattern in Checkers.

ENDGAME TECHNIQUE: When few pieces remain, the board is wide open and Kings dominate. A single King versus a single King is usually a draw — neither can force a capture. Two Kings versus one King is a win if you use them together to restrict the opponent's King into a corner or an edge (called the squeeze). Never separate your Kings in the endgame — they are strongest working together to cut off escape routes.

THE OPPOSITION: When two Kings face each other on the same diagonal, the player who does not have to move often has the advantage — moving first means giving ground. This concept, called the opposition, is critical in endgame positions. Sometimes the best move is the one that forces your opponent to move their King first.

metadata.notes: Public domain game for Dice Tower West demo. Covers standard American/English Checkers (8x8 board, forced captures). Strategy section enriched with king strategy, forced capture tactics, and endgame technique per ticket requirements. Source: World Checkers/Draughts Federation official rules. All content original — no rulebook text reproduced.
